coredata.dc:title: Machine learning-assisted wide-gamut fluorescence visual test paper for propazine determination in fish and seawater samples
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hua
You, Jinjie
Liu, Chenxi
Zhang, Zeming
Sun, Aili
Hao, Guijie
Shi, Xizhi
coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP-QDs) with fluorescence quenching ability toward propazine was synthesized for propazine detection. b(Blue)-MIP-QDs were prepared using ZnCdS/ZnS QDs via reverse micro-emulsion, whereas r(red)-MIP-QDs were synthesized using CdSe/ZnS QDs. By utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a stable fluorescence intensity reference, the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper was constructed on the basis of mixing b-MIP-QDs, r-MIP-QDs, and GQDs under the optimal ratio. When analyzing spiked propazine in fish and seawater samples using a test paper, satisfactory recoveries of 104.0 %–114.6 % and 92.0 %–96.4 % were obtained, with corresponding limits of detection of 5.0 μg/kg and 1.0 μg/L, respectively. The RGB extractor was utilized to extract the actual fluorescence color and construct a dataset consisting of R, G, and B values, as well as concentration data from 400 samples. The SVR model of Python 3.9.7 was used to obtain and analyze the concentration and feature data. After optimization, the constructed model achieved a correlation coefficient of 0.98 and an RMSE of only 1.81, indicating high prediction accuracy and excellent generalization ability that meet quenching prediction requirements. As an intelligent and rapid detection method, this model holds significant practical significance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Propazine
Molecularly imprinted
Fluorescence sensor
Visual detection
Machine learning

Fabrication of wide gamut fluorescence test paper

Optimizing the measurement condition of the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor
Construction and characterization of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper
Selectivity evaluation of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper
Practical application and performance comparison of the developed method
Machine learning assisted the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor
Mechanism of the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Healthy Freshwater Aquaculture, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Afairs (ZJK202205); the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo (2022S163); the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2021Z056); the Basic Public Welfare Research Program of Zhejiang Province (LGC22C190001); the Pioneer and Leading Goose R&D Program of Zhejiang (2023C02038); the Graduate Research and Innovation project of Ningbo University (IF2024061).

This research was funded by the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Healthy Freshwater Aquaculture, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Afairs (ZJK202205); the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo (2022S163); the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2021Z056); the basic Public Welfare Research Program of Zhejiang Province (LGC22C190001); the \"Pioneer\" and \"Leading Goose\" R&D Program of Zhejiang (2022C02028, 2023C02038); the Graduate Research and Innovation project of Ningbo University (IF2024061).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of the r-MIP-QDs, b-MIP-QDs, and GQDs.
Response time (A) b-MIP-QDs; (B) r-MIP-QDs. Acetic acid:(C) b-M/NIP-QDs; (D) r-M/NIP-QDs. Percentage of water: (E) b-M/NIP-QDs; (F) r-M/NIP-QDs. Buffer: (G) b-M/NIP-QDs; (H) r-M/NIP-QDs.

Fluorescence spectra for promazine detection from 0 mg/L to 0.2 mg/L. (A) b-MIP-QDs: r-MIP-QDs=1:1, (B) GQDs: b-MIP-QDs=1:1, (C) GQDs: r-MIP-QDs=1:1. Fluorescence sensor for promazine detection from 0 mg/L to 0.5 mg/L. (D) b-MIP-QDs: gQDs: r-MIP-QDs=5:5:5; (E) b-MIP-QDs: GQDs: r-MIP-QDs=9:5:14; (F) b-MIP-QDs: gQDs: r-MIP-QDs=9:5:9.
The selectivity of wide-gamut fluorescence system.
The linear of wide-gamut fluorescence system for promazine detection.
The SVM model construction (A); Concentration chromaticity diagram of wide-gamut fluorescence system (B).
The confusion matrices of seawater samples.
Schematic of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper for propazine.
The practical application of wide gamut fluorescence system for propazine determination in seawater and fish samples.

head.ce:title: Machine learning-assisted wide-gamut fluorescence visual test paper for propazine determination in fish and seawater samples

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Marine Sciences Ningbo University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP-QDs) with fluorescence quenching ability toward propazine was synthesized for propazine detection. b(Blue)-MIP-QDs were prepared using ZnCdS/ZnS QDs via reverse micro-emulsion, whereas r(red)-MIP-QDs were synthesized using CdSe/ZnS QDs. By utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a stable fluorescence intensity reference, the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper was constructed on the basis of mixing b-MIP-QDs, r-MIP-QDs, and GQDs under the optimal ratio. When analyzing spiked propazine in fish and seawater samples using a test paper, satisfactory recoveries of 104.0 %–114.6 % and 92.0 %–96.4 % were obtained, with corresponding limits of detection of 5.0 μg/kg and 1.0 μg/L, respectively. The RGB extractor was utilized to extract the actual fluorescence color and construct a dataset consisting of R, G, and B values, as well as concentration data from 400 samples. The SVR model of Python 3.9.7 was used to obtain and analyze the concentration and feature data. After optimization, the constructed model achieved a correlation coefficient of 0.98 and an RMSE of only 1.81, indicating high prediction accuracy and excellent generalization ability that meet quenching prediction requirements. As an intelligent and rapid detection method, this model holds significant practical significance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Triazine herbicides, which are known for their potent broad-spectrum weed control properties, are the extensively used herbicides globally, effectively targeting broad-leaved, grass weeds, and algae [1]. Propazine, also identified as 2,4-bis(isopropylamino)-6-chloro-1,3,5-triazine, stands out as a widely utilized triazine herbicide [2]. Given its stability, resistance to degradation, and extended half-life, propazine could accumulate in crops [3]. Prolonged exposure to triazine herbicides and their degradation products poses a potential chronic health risk for humans, which can infiltrate the ecosystem through soil and water, adversely impacting soil microorganisms, aquatic organisms, and wildlife. Studies have detected propazine residues in birds, mammals, and groundwater [4]. Triazine herbicides have been linked to disruptions in the human endocrine and immune systems, with prolonged accumulation potentially leading to chronic health issues such as an elevated risk of cancer and reproductive toxicity [5]. Traditional methods for detecting triazine herbicide residues in food and environmental samples typically involve liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry [6] and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [7,8]. Although these methods are efficient for monitoring, they often require expensive equipment, complex sample preparation procedures, and skilled operators. In addition, they rely on high-precision instrumentation to confirm suspected species, making them unsuitable for immediate field testing and rapid on-site monitoring. Therefore, developing a detection method that is sensitive, accurate, and rapid for determining promazine is crucial. Such a method would ensure food safety by providing a practical solution that can be easily implemented in various settings.

Visual detection technology, such as fluorescence sensors [9,10], nanoprobes [11,12], and nanometer materials [13], has excellent detection capabilities and evident color changes, providing a convenient and sensitive detection platform crucial for applications in food safety, clinical diagnosis, environmental monitoring, and others. Fluorescence sensing, which selectively detects target analytes through mechanisms such as fluorescence enhancement [14,15], quenching [16], or spectral alteration [17], has become a leading technology in optical sensor visual detection. This development marks a revolutionary shift in optical fluorescence sensors, particularly in the realm of nano-fluorescent materials. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) feature specific binding chambers that are complementary in shape, size, and functional groups, enabling receptor-specific recognition [18,19]. These polymers provide distinct advantages in selectivity and sensitivity when detecting trace substances within complex substrates. Quantum dots (QDs), as classical nanomaterials [20], exhibit unique optical properties, including wide excitation and narrow emission spectra, surpassing traditional fluorescent proteins and organic dyes in fluorescence characteristics [21]. When integrated into a QDs-molecular imprinting sensor, it provides a naked-eye visual signal with a low detection limit, enabling sensitive, intuitive, and efficient analyte identification [22]. This technology shows great potential in biosensing [23], practical applications [24], and medical diagnosis [25], particularly in the rapid detection of seawater and foodstuff safety [26,27].

The current development of polymer sensors for visual inspection primarily focuses on “single emission” modes [28], where fluorescence intensity increases or diminishes upon analyte recognition. However, challenges arise from background fluorescence, instrument fluctuations, and operating parameters, making it difficult to observe subtle fluorescence intensity changes visually. By contrast, the MIP sensor with dual fluorescence peaks [29,30], which is introduced by incorporating two types of fluorescent substances during MIP preparation, overcomes this limitation [30]. During analyte identification, the fluorescence emission intensity of these two types does not change synchronously, leading to a change in fluorescence color, thereby providing a self-correction function [31]. Combining single emission and double emission, a wide color gamut sensor with high sensitivity and an internal correction function has been developed. This advancement remarkably mitigates background interference caused by biological and environmental matrices during the detection process. The wide color gamut emission system, utilizing red, green, and blue (RGB) fluorescence modes [32], provides a diverse fluorescence range. Notably, no previous reports have mentioned the detection of promazine using a wide color gamut fluorescence sensor and rapid visual test paper, indicating a pioneering avenue for future research and applications in the field.

To enhance detection efficiency, introducing a level of intelligence into the detection process is necessary. At the heart of artificial intelligence, machine learning possesses unparalleled advantages in the realms of data mining [33], analysis [34], and prediction [35]. Machine learning, as a model-building and optimization method, is based on statistical learning theory [36]. It involves the use of algorithms and techniques to train models on existing datasets, enabling them to learn patterns and make predictions on new, unseen data in the context of color analysis [37]. By learning from this data, the machine learning model can then predict the properties and characteristics of unknown color values [38]. In addition, machine learning can be applied to color classification and recognition tasks. For example, it can be used to classify different colors into categories such as cool and warm tones [39,40]. Machine learning models can also learn associations between certain colors and emotional states, allowing them to generalize and predict the emotional impact of specific colors.

Support vector regression (SVR) is a linear classifier used in binary classification tasks within the realm of supervised learning [41,42], which offers precise solutions to binary classification problems by skillfully balancing empirical and structural risk considerations. It represents a pivotal facet of the broader support vector machine (SVM) framework, which is characterized by its exceptional stability and performance [43]. Its inherent stability makes it a robust choice for addressing complex machine learning. SVR utilizes the principles of SVM to find an optimal hyperplane that fits the data by transforming it into a high-dimensional space [44]. The SVR model is trained by minimizing the error between the predicted values and the true values in the training data, which are achieved by using kernel functions that can capture complex non-linear relationships within the data. The SVR model has demonstrated excellent performance in prediction and classification tasks. It also shows application potential in various fields, including finance, medical treatment, and weather forecasting [45,46]. Its ability to handle non-linear data and capture intricate relationships makes it a valuable tool for analyzing and predicting complex phenomena. Thus, this research aims to leverage its regression capabilities to facilitate the prediction of RGB values and concentration by SVR, potentially providing valuable insights and innovative solutions in the field of rate-fluorescence sensor strategy modeling [47]. Notably, existing literature that specifically mentions the application of SVR in the development of a prediction model for the rate-fluorescence sensor RGB strategy is lacking, although the SVR is extremely important throughout the actual scenario.

In this study, a fluorescence sensor platform with a broad color gamut was introduced, leveraging fluorescence-assisted molecular imprinting technology for the selective detection of promazine. Initially, r-MIP-QDs and b-MIP-QDs were synthesized using CdSe/ZnS QDs (red light) and ZnCdS/ZnS QDs (blue light) through inverse micro-emulsion. The exploitation of the wide color gamut display enhances visual color performance via gamut expansion, ensuring that the fluorescence sensor platform meets stringent requirements for high accuracy, precision, and sensitivity in rapid detection. Optimal proportions of b-MIP-QDs, r-MIP-QDs, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs, green light) were used to construct a fluorescence sensing paper with excellent linearity, accuracy, precision, and sensitivity. The wide color gamut fluorescent sensing paper exhibited remarkable color changes (ranging from rosy red to light orange red, from dark orange to olive, and ultimately to dark green) corresponding to different concentrations of promazine, showcasing exceptional sensitivity. The integration of a machine learning algorithm (SVR) with a three-color sensing platform facilitated model training on existing data, enabling pattern recognition and prediction of unknown color values. This approach has been proven successful in achieving rapid, sensitive, and accurate determination of promazine in fish and seawater samples.

Propazine, atrazine, propyzamide, isoproturon and prometryn were purchased from Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Augsburg, Germany). Triton X-100 was from Solibao (Beijing, China). 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), methacrylic acid (MAA), ethylene glycol methacrylate (EGDMA), and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). ZnCdS/ZnS QDs and CdSe/ZnS QDs were purchased from BEIDA JUBANG Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). GQDs (green light) were purchased from Pioneer Nano Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Ultra-pure water purification system (Xi'an, China) prepared deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm).

Fluorescence Spectrophotometer F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan) was used for fluorescence intensity. UV-3300 Spectrophotometer (MAPADA, China) was used for recording the absorption spectra. Scanning Electron Microscope S-4800 (Hitachi, Japan) and Transmission Electron Microscope H-7650 (Hitachi, Japan) were used to observe the morphology of MIP-QDs, NIP-QDs, and GQDs. Elemental analysis was recorded by an Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS, JSM-6701 F). FT-IR was used to record chemical bonds on the surface of polymers by Fourier Infrared Spectroscopy Nicolet 6700 (Thermo, USA).

Propazine-imprinted polymers were synthesized using the sol-gel method following a previously reported procedure [48]. Cyclohexane (10.0 mL), Tritone-X 100 (2.4 mL), and AIBN (40 mg) were added into a 50.0 mL round bottom flask and stirred for 15 min at room temperature. Then, ZnCdS/ZnS QDs (blue), CdSe/ZnS QDs (red, 100.0 μL, 5.0 mg/mL), TEOS (50.0 μL), and NH3·H2O (100.0 μL) were successively added and heated to 60 °C for 2 h with 350 r/min to form QD seeds. Propazine (137.5 μL, 20 mg/mL), APTES (46.6 μL), MAA (6.8 μL), and EGDMA (40.0 μL) were added and stirred for 2 h before being transferred into the QD seed solution. After stirring for 8 h at 60 °C under dark conditions, 10.0 mL of acetone and 5.0 mL of ultra-pure water were added in turn to remove the unreacted reagent under ultrasound oscillating. Finally, a mixture of ethanol:acetonitrile (8:2, v/v, 6.0 mL) was added to remove the template until the fluorescence intensity no longer increased to form the MIP-QDs (r- and b-MIP-QDs). Their corresponding non-imprinted polymer NIP-QDs (r- and b-NIP-QDs) were prepared by using the same step without the template.

The optical properties of MIP-QDs, NIP-QDs, and wide-gamut test paper for propazine have been characterized by using an F-4600 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrum was measured at 340 nm with excitation slit widths (5 nm) and emission slit widths (10 nm) at a tube voltage of 750 eV. Measurements were processed by adding MIP-QD solutions (20.0 mg/L) and propazine (5.0 mg/L) performed after incubating for 120 s. The control experiments were simultaneously performed using the same procedure. All experiments were tested in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the wide-gamut fluorescence system
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of MIPs for propazine, the chemical structural analogs of propazine such as atrazine, prometryn, isoproturon, and acetylphenylamine were selected. The Stern–Volmer equation (F0/F=1+KSV[Q]) was used to calculate the factor of the wide-gamut fluorescence system, where F0 and F represent the I 510/(I 450+I 630) values before and after the addition of a quencher; Ksv is the quenching constant; Q is the quencher concentration, and I 450, I 510, and I 630 represent the fluorescence value of the b-MIP-QDs, r-MIP-QDs, and GQDs mixtures at 450, 510, and 630 nm emission wavelengths, respectively. The selectivity of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper was evaluated by the fluorescence color evolution under different quenching conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of wide gamut fluorescence test paper
ce:section.ce:para: A nylon film was carefully cut into a circular shape with a diameter of 0.5 cm and then immersed in a solution with optimized proportions and test conditions (acetonitrile and water in a ratio of 6:4, with an acetic acid concentration of 3 % and a PBS concentration of 4 mM) to enhance the absorption of fluorescent substances effectively. The nylon film was dried at 50 °C for 2 h to enhance the stability and longevity of the fluorescent test paper. The preparation conditions of the two-color fluorescent test paper are the same as those of the three-color fluorescent test paper, except for the proportion and concentration of RGB fluorescent substances. The prepared fluorescent test papers were stored in a refrigerator set at 4 °C in the dark.

Seawater samples were collected from Hangzhou Bay and purified using a 0.45 μm cellulose acetate membrane. Then, the samples were stored at 4 °C. Fish samples were also obtained from Hangzhou Bay. Typical fish samples weighing 5.0 g were homogenized for 5 min at a speed of 8.0 × 103 rpm to produce surimi. After allowing the mixture to stand for 2 h, 10.0 mL of acetonitrile and 1.5 mL of sodium chloride were added. The samples were vortexed for 5 min and then centrifuged at 4.0 × 103 g for 10 min to obtain the supernatant. This step was repeated once. The supernatants were combined, and 10.0 mL of n-hexane:acetonitrile (v/v, 10:1) was added. After vortexing for 1 min, the samples were centrifuged at 4.0 × 103 g for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was evaporated to dryness under an N2 atmosphere at room temperature. Finally, 1.0 mL of cyclohexane was added to dissolve the residues, and the mixture was filtered using a 0.45 μm cellulose acetate membrane for subsequent analysis.

In this study, a digital camera was used to capture images illuminated by 365 nm UV lamps. An RGB extractor was utilized to extract the actual fluorescence colors from the images, forming a comprehensive dataset. This dataset consisted of the R, G, and B values of the fluorescence colors and their corresponding concentrations, encompassing a total of 400 samples. To analyze and mine the concentration and characteristic data (RGB), Python 3.9.7 was utilized on a Windows 10 system equipped with a 2.5 GHz Intel Core i5–7300HQ processor. The SVR model was implemented to establish an RGB model for predicting the concentration. Model performance was evaluated using the correlation coefficient (R) and root mean square error (RMSE). In this experiment, the SVR linear kernel function was utilized. The function source codes of the implementation details are provided in text S1 in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the wide-gamut fluorescence system
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of MIPs (r-MIP-QDs and b-MIP-QDs) is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The structures were confirmed by SEM, TEM, FT-IR, EDS, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The characterization data are given in the Electronic Supplementary Information (Figs. S1–S4). CdSe/ZnS QDs with red emission and ZnCdS/ZnS QDs with blue emission were used to create the blue and red fluorescence emissions of MIP-QDs, respectively, based on the RGB principle. Green QDs (GQDs) were selected as the internal standard for the wide-gamut fluorescence system because of their good water solubility and high stability. Under UV light, the MIP-QDs emitted a rosy red glow, which was different from the individual colors of r-MIP-QDs, b-MIP-QDs, and GQDs ( Fig. 1, insert figure). The UV–vis spectra of the three-color mixture ( Fig. S4 ) showed that all three types of QDs had maximum absorption peaks near 340 nm. As shown in Fig. 1, b-MIP-QDs, GQDs, and r-MIP-QDs exhibited fluorescence emissions at 450, 510, and 630 nm, respectively, when excited at 340 nm. This result indicates a clear distinction in maximum fluorescence emission among r-MIP-QDs, b-MIP-QDs, and GQDs. The results indicated no significant difference in fluorescence emission intensity between mixed and unmixed samples, and an excitation wavelength of 340 nm was found to be optimal for preparing a wide-gamut fluorescence sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the measurement condition of the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As the main factor for the measurement conditions of the wide-gamut fluorescence system, the response time of the wide-gamut fluorescence system to detect promazine was first scanned from 1 s to 300 s. As shown in Figs. 2A and 2B, after adding promazine, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 510/I 450 (GQDs/b-MIP-QDs) and I 510/I 630 (GQDs/r-MIP-QDs) increased immediately and reached dynamic equilibrium at 120 s. This result indicates that the adsorption/dissociation of promazine in the wide-gamut fluorescence system reaches equilibrium. The wide-gamut fluorescence system has a selective recognition time of 120 s for promazine. Such a rapid response is due to the hydrophobicity of the SiO2 imprinting layer of MIPs and the combined effect of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic attraction between the binding site and the analyte in the imprinting chamber. Therefore, 120 s was selected as the response time in the subsequent experiment.

The influence of varying acidity levels (0–5 %) on the selective fluorescence quenching of MIP-QDs was investigated using the fluorescence intensity ratio F(450)0/F(450) and F(630)0/F(630), with the results presented in Fig. 2C and Fig. 2D. b-MIP-QDs achieved the optimal fluorescence quenching effect when the organic acid ratio was 2 %, with an imprinting factor value of 4.7 (Fig. 2C). Conversely, r-MIP-QDs exhibited the most favorable fluorescence response at an organic acid content of 3 %, with an imprinting factor value of 3.15 (Fig. 2D). The variation in acidity affected the three-dimensional structure of the imprinted cavity and the morphology of surface functional groups. In addition, strong acids and bases led to surface defects in the imprinted silicon layer, which in turn influenced its surface charge. These factors had a remarkable impact on the selective fluorescence quenching capabilities of b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs toward promazine. As summarized in Table S1, the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor has the best specific recognition effect on propazine at 3 % acetic acid content. Therefore, the final test condition was selected as 3 % organic acid content. Considering the good fluorescence intensity and potential application in food samples, 3 % organic acid content was selected as the appropriate level for subsequent experiments.

The moisture content of the detection conditions has a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of MIP-QDs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency and intensity factor of b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs are optimal, which can be expressed using the F(450)0/F(450) and F(630)0/F(630), when the water content in the acetonitrile solution is 40 % (Figs. 2E and 2F). This finding indicates that hydrophobic interactions play a crucial role in the specific recognition among the imprinting sites of MIP-QDs and propazine. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of r-MIP-QDs and b-MIP-QDs is consistent with their IF because they are synthesized using the same methods, and the characteristics and number of imprinting sites on their surfaces are highly similar. By contrast, the fluorescence quenching degree of b-NIP-QDs and r-NIP-QDs (non-imprinted polymers) remains almost unchanged compared with b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs because the surface of MIP-QDs possesses specific binding cavities, whereas the surface of b-NIP-QDs and r-NIP-QDs lacks such specific binding cavities. Therefore, a solvent mixture of acetonitrile and water in a ratio of 6:4 was selected for subsequent experiments.

The buffer concentration not only impacts the specific recognition between the imprinting sites of MIP-QDs and template molecules but also exerts a certain influence on the dynamic electron transfer fluorescence quenching between QDs and template molecules. The impact of buffer salt concentrations on the performance of b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs was studied. As shown in Fig. 2G and Fig. 2H, the optimal fluorescence quenching effect was achieved for b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs at a PBS concentration of 4 mM. Based on previous optimization conditions, the IF factor increased to 7.18 and 3.22, respectively. Therefore, a PBS concentration of 4 mM was selected for the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper
ce:section.ce:para: The difference in color change observed by the naked eye is more pronounced than the difference in intensity change. To establish a wide-color gamut fluorescence sensing system, the measurement conditions of r-MIP-QDs to promazine are optimized, and the selectivity and competitive activity were investigated (Fig. S8,9). The conventional single emission mode detects the target analyte by increasing or decreasing fluorescence emission, which is not easily discernible to the naked eye and can be influenced by background interference, instrument fluctuations, and operational parameters. We conducted a dual-emission fluorescence system by mixing the MIPs and GQDs, which has self-correction capabilities where varying quenching efficiencies of two emission peaks result in evident changes in fluorescence color under identical excitation wavelengths. Fig. 3 illustrates the fluorescence emission spectrum of promazine (concentration range: 0–0.2 mg/L) detected by the dual-emission fluorescence sensor. As shown in Fig. 3A, when b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs are mixed equally for propazine detection, minimal change in fluorescence color is observed with the naked eye. This finding may be attributed to the remarkable decrease in fluorescence intensity of b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs upon specific binding with propazine but limited alteration in the overall fluorescence color because of similar quenching rates. Similarly, when b-MIP-QDs and GQDs are simultaneously mixed for specific propazine detection (Fig. 3B), a transition from blue to blue green and eventually cyan is observed with the naked eye. This phenomenon could be explained by close proximity between blue emissions and green emissions resulting in a narrow window for blue-green transition that makes significant color changes difficult to observe visually. When r-MIP-QDs and GQDs are combined equally (red-green window), a distinct orange-blue color shift can be readily perceived with naked eye observation (Fig. 3C).

Considering that only red-green color change was achieved with the mixture of r-MIP-QDs and GQDs, b-MIP-QDs were used to provide blue fluorescence emission and expand the range of color discoloration. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence of b-MIP-QDs (450 nm) and r-MIP-QDs (630 nm) gradually diminished, whereas the fluorescence of GQDs (510 nm) remained stable when a mixture of b-MIP-QDs:GQDs:r-MIP-QDs was specifically used for promazine detection at a ratio of 5:5:5. Moreover, no significant color change was observed in the fluorescence paper with varying concentrations of promazine (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mg/L). Given the poor red fluorescence emission in the fluorescent paper, the proportion of b-MIP-QDs:GQDs:r-MIP-QDs was adjusted to 9:5:14. The recognition of propazine rapidly quenched the red emission peaks, resulting in the occupation of the imprinted cavities in r-MIP-QDs and a color variation from rosy red to yellowish-brown observed in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3E). These results demonstrated an increase in the fraction of r-MIP-QDs from 33 % to 50 % and an increase in red fluorescence caused by propazine recognition with no remarkable expansion of the color evolution range. When the ratio of b-MIP-QDs, GQDs, and r-MIP-QDs was adjusted to 9:5:9, the fluorescence intensity decreased in ratio because of the difference in quenching rate. The wide-gamut fluorescence system can provide abundant fluorescence color changes (from light pink to light salmon to dark orange to olive and finally to dark green), which were observed even in the presence of low-concentration propazine. The color of the prepared wide-gamut fluorescence test paper was changed by trace propazine from 1 to 200 mg/L, and the fluorescence color evolution from light pink to light salmon to dark orange to olive and finally to dark green was obtained (inset of Fig. 3F). Then, the proportion of b-MIP-QDs:GQDs:r-MIP-QDs accounting for 9:5:9 was used in the fluorescent sensor construction for further experiments to detect wild fields of propazine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity evaluation of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper
ce:section.ce:para: Multicolor fluorescence emission can expand the color range and display a richer array of color variations. Thus, we have developed a wide-gamut fluorescence sensor and its corresponding fluorescence test paper based on the RGB strategy. The wide-gamut fluorescence visual test paper provides high selectivity, sensitivity, and self-correction, enabling observation of color changes under UV light with the naked eye. In evaluating the selectivity of the wide-gamut fluorescence visual test paper for promazine, propazine and its structural analogs (atrazine, propionamide, isopropanone, and prometryn) were subjected to fluorescence quenching and fluorescence emission profile scanning at the same concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4, the addition of atamazine, primezin, benzoynamide, and isopropyrone did not result in neither remarkable fluorescence quenching ((I450+I630)/I510), nor evident change in fluorescence color that is visible to the naked eye within the wide-gamut fluorescence system. However, when propazine was added to the system, a substantial decrease in fluorescence intensity occurred along with a shift from rosy red to green fluorescence color on the test paper. Neither the NIP-QD-based wide-gamut fluorescence sensor nor the test paper exhibited significant changes in fluorescent signal intensity and color upon exposure. The high selectivity observed with MIP-QDs can be attributed to their specific molecularly imprinted cavities, whereas NIP-QDs lack such specific recognition sites on their surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application and performance comparison of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the developed test paper was studied in seawater and fish samples spiked with different concentrations of propazine. The original fluorescence color of the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper changed from rosy red to pink to orange to yellow and finally to dark green under ultraviolet light of 365 nm, which is easy to observe by the naked eye. As shown in Fig. 5, the ratio of fluorescence intensities, I 510/(I 450+I 630), showed excellent linearity against propazine concentrations in the range of 0.005–2.000 mg/L with a correlation coefficient of R2= 0.9941. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.001 mg/L is obtained on the basis of the IUPAC standard (LOD=3σ/s), where σ is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, and s is the slope of the linear calibration [49].

Furthermore, to evaluate its practical application in propazine determination, recovery experiments in seawater and fish samples were conducted using the wide-gamut fluorescence system ( Table 1). No propazine was detected in seawater and fish samples, and three levels of 5.0, 15.0, and 50.0 μg/L were spiked in seawater and fish samples. The recoveries of seawater and fish samples were in the range of 92.0 %–96.4 % and 104.0 %–114.6 % with RSDs<6.3 %, respectively, which indicated that the fabricated wide-gamut fluorescence system had good applicability with acceptable accuracy and precision in propazine determination in real samples. The LOD of the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor was 5.0 μg/kg and 1.0 μg/L in fish and seawater samples, respectively. Moreover, the wide-gamut fluorescence test paper at 5.0 μg/L showed an evident naked-eye discernible fluorescence color change in spiked seawater and fish samples. These results indicated that the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor and visual test paper could be successfully applied to the determination of trace propazine in actual samples.

The construction strategy and performance of the developed wide-gamut fluorescence system for propazine detection were compared with those of the previously reported molecularly imprinted fluorescence approach. As given in Table S2, compared with other methods, the developed wide-gamut fluorescence system had evident color changes, a wider range of discoloration (from light pink to light salmon to dark orange to olive and finally to dark green), lower detection limits (1μg/L), less time-consuming (2 min) process, and outstanding imprinting factor (IF=6.98) at low concentrations, indicating that the multicolor emission platform exhibited the sensitive and convenient determination of propazine in real seawater and fish samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning assisted the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the accuracy of the prediction model, 70 % of the dataset was utilized for training, whereas the remaining 30 % of the data was reserved for SVR prediction, with samples not present in the dataset serving as the testing set ( Fig. 6A). We utilized a smartphone to gather the image results, subsequently employing RGB feature extraction techniques (Text S2) to convert the RGB values of the fluorescence image. Following this, we analyzed the pertinent RGB values using the SVR model to determine the corresponding concentrations. The model data is graphically represented in Fig. 6B. The concentration quantification, which is represented by the concentration chromaticity diagram of the wide-gamut fluorescence system, is executed upon the collection of fluorescence images. Following optimization, the constructed model achieved an impressive correlation coefficient of 0.98, with a minimal RMSE of only 1.81. Furthermore, an independent test set comprising 70 samples, which were not part of the original data set was collected. This independent test set was used to evaluate the model’s predictive performance. Therefore, the model is well suited for predicting the concentration of promazine in real sample. According to certain pertinent articles, machine learning has been utilized in fluorescence analysis and prediction (Tab. S3). It is evident that the machine learning algorithm demonstrates remarkable predictive performance and precision in the realm of fluorescence prediction and analysis.

To validate the predictive accuracy of the model, seawater samples with propazine concentrations (0–2000 µg/L) were randomly segmented into 10 distinct groups as independent samples. Subsequently, fluorescence images of the actual samples were scrutinized to assess the method’s predictive capability for propazine concentration. As shown in Fig. 7, the experimental findings reveal that all identified samples are along the diagonal matrix. This alignment indicates a high level of concordance between the model’s predictive outcomes and the actual measurement results. Then, the receiver operating characteristic curves were plotted with a true positive rate on the y-axis and a false positive rate on the x-axis to evaluate the model. The results in Fig. S7 demonstrated high prediction accuracy of the SVR model, indicating strong agreement between predicted and actual concentrations of propazine in seawater samples. This suggests the model's reliability in assessing propazine concentrations and exceptional accuracy in prediction with remarkable generalization capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the wide-gamut fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the quenching mechanism [50] ( Fig. 8), the fluorescence quenching of b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs by propazine is due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET). PET is a process in which excited electrons from an electron donor (b- and r-MIP-QDs) are transferred to an electron acceptor (propazine) in the excited state. This transfer occurs when the QDs are excited by photons, causing the electrons in the ground-state orbits of the QDs to be excited to the highest occupied molecular orbital higher energy orbits, which allows them to transfer to the electron acceptor, which corresponds to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. The UV–vis absorption spectra obtained from a UV–vis spectrophotometer show that b-, r-MIP-QDs, and GQDs do not exhibit remarkable UV absorption, indicating no energy transfer between them. The lack of overlap between the emission spectra of the QDs and the UV–visible absorption spectrum of propazine rules out resonance energy transfer as the main quenching mechanism. In addition, the non-overlapping ultraviolet absorption peak of propazine and the fluorescence excitation wavelength of the QDs exclude fluorescence quenching caused by the internal filtering effect. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching observed in b-MIP-QDs and r-MIP-QDs when interacting with propazine is primarily due to dynamic quenching through non-radiative surface channels resulting from surface electron transfer.

In this study, we developed a new wide-gamut fluorescence sensing system based on three primary colors and created a visual sensing paper for the selective and sensitive detection of promazine. This paper also shows a distinct color change (rosy red to yellow green) because of the different fluorescence quenching rates of blue and red when reacting with promazine. It exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity, with continuous color changes as the promazine concentration increases. We successfully applied this visual sensing paper for rapid promazine detection in seawater and fish samples, achieving recoveries of 104.0 %–114.6 % and 92.0 %–96.4 % and detection limits of 5.0 μg/kg and 1.0 μg/L, respectively. To improve concentration prediction, we constructed an RGB model by combining linear kernel functions of a SVM. After optimization, the model achieved a correlation coefficient of 0.98 and an RMSE of 1.81. The model demonstrated excellent agreement between predicted and actual concentrations in 70 independent test sets, showcasing high accuracy and generalization ability. This model has practical applications in environmental monitoring, food safety, and related fields.

Chenxi Liu: Data curation, Investigation. Zeming Zhang: Supervision, Investigation. Aili Sun: Visualization, Investigation. Hua Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jinjie You: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Guijie Hao: Writing – review & editing. Xizhi Shi: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Healthy Freshwater Aquaculture, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Afairs (ZJK202205); the Public Welfare Research Project of Ningbo (2022S163); the Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2021Z056); the Basic Public Welfare Research Program of Zhejiang Province (LGC22C190001); the Pioneer and Leading Goose R&D Program of Zhejiang (2023C02038); the Graduate Research and Innovation project of Ningbo University (IF2024061).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of support vector machine (SVM) learning in cancer genomics, Cancer Genom

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.J. Huang, N.G. Cai, P.P. Pacheco, S. Narrandes, Y. Wang, W. Xu, Applications of support vector machine (SVM) learning in cancer genomics, Cancer Genom. Proteom. 15 (2018) 41-51.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Discovery of DNA-carbon nanotube sensors for serotonin with machine learning and near-infrared fluorescence spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Kelich, S. Jeong, N. Navarro, J. Adams, X.Q. Sun, H.H. Zhao, M.P. Landry, L. Vukovic, Discovery of DNA-carbon nanotube sensors for serotonin with machine learning and near-infrared fluorescence spectroscopy, ACS Nano 16 (2022) 736-745.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advancement of carbon nanomaterials engrained molecular imprinted polymer for environmental matrix

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Singh, S. Singh, S.P. Singh, S.S. Patel, Recent advancement of carbon nanomaterials engrained molecular imprinted polymer for environmental matrix, Trends Environ. Anal. 27 (2020), e00092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A single dual-emissive nanofluorophore test paper for highly sensitive colorimetry-based quantification of blood glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Huang, Y.J. Zhou, C. Liu, R.L. Zhang, L.Y. Zhang, S.H. Du, B.H. Liu, M.Y. Han, Z.P. Zhang, A single dual-emissive nanofluorophore test paper for highly sensitive colorimetry-based quantification of blood glucose, Biosens. Bioelectron. 86 (2016) 530-535.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence quenching of photoluminescent organic polymer nanofilms by ferric ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.X. Feng, Z. Xu, C.G. Qi, X.M. Guo, L.G. Gai, Fluorescence quenching of photoluminescent organic polymer nanofilms by ferric ions, Microchem. J. 154 (2020) 104639.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Liu Hua received her Dr's degree from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the applications of fluorescent probes and fluorescence sensing in environmental and biological diagnostics.

You Jinjie received his Master´s degree from Ningbo University in 2022. He is currently studying for his Ph.D. at Ningbo University. His research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence and colorimetric sensors and their applications in the environment and organisms.

Liu Chenxi received her Master´s degree from Ningbo University in 2020. She is currently studying for his Ph.D. at South China University of Technology. Her research is mainly concerned with the construction of fluorescence sensors and their applications of aquatic products.

Zhang Zeming received his BS and Ph.D. degree from Ocean University of China in 2013 and 2018. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the detection, migration and transformation of organicpollutants.

Sun Aili received her Dr's degree from Ningbo University in 2017. She is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. Her research interests concern on the development and application of fluorescence sensors for food safety.

Hao Guijie received her Dr's degree from Ningbo University. She is currently a faculty in Zhejiang institute of freshwater fisheries. Her research interests concern on quality safety and processing of aquatic products.

Shi Xizhi received his Dr's degree from Shanghai Jiaotong University in 2008. He is currently a faculty in School of Marine Sciences at Ningbo University. His research interests concern on the development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.
